title: After Theory
excerpt: Twenty years after Literary Theory, Eagleton returns to assess the legacy of cultural theory and argue that the golden age of high theory is over — and that a new, more ambitious kind of thinking is urgently needed.
keyIdeas: The golden age of cultural theory (1960s-1990s) has ended
Postmodernism's relativism is politically toothless in an age of global terror and inequality
Cultural theory must return to fundamental questions about truth, morality, and human nature
The body, death, evil, and human vulnerability are neglected topics in recent theory
Theory needs to reconnect with the grand narratives it dismissed
keyTakeaways: Postmodern skepticism about truth and morality leaves us unable to oppose tyranny effectively
The retreat from metanarratives was a luxury the post-9/11 world cannot afford
Cultural theory's focus on difference and identity has obscured the continuing importance of class
The most pressing intellectual questions today are ethical and existential, not merely textual
whoShouldRead: Readers of Literary Theory: An Introduction who want to know what Eagleton thinks happened to theory
Critics of postmodernism seeking a sophisticated critique
Anyone wondering what comes 'after' the age of high theory
whoShouldSkip: Those who believe the postmodern turn was entirely positive
Readers seeking a comprehensive survey — this is a polemic, not a textbook
